unit.name: Learning-rate schedules, second-order optimization, and the neural-network transition
unit.description: Covers learning-rate tuning and schedules, Hessian-based methods, practical optimizer defaults, and the preview of neural networks.
unit.summary: All discussed optimizers have learning rate as a hyperparameter [ts=3525s]. A very high learning rate can make loss explode by oscillating out of the landscape, a very low learning rate converges slowly, and a still-too-high rate near a minimum can keep the optimizer bouncing around [ts=3556s]. Modern deep learning often varies learning rate during training [ts=3609s]. Step decay reduces the learning rate at fixed points, such as multiplying by 0.1 during ResNet training [ts=3625s]. Cosine decay follows a half-cosine from maximum learning rate toward zero; other options include linear and inverse-square-root decay [ts=3654s]. Linear warmup ramps from zero to the maximum learning rate before another scheduler, and a common empirical rule is to scale learning rate by N when batch size increases by N [ts=3726s]. Second-order optimization uses the Hessian to form a quadratic approximation instead of only a gradient-based linear approximation, but Hessian methods are rarely used for large neural networks because second derivatives and Hessian matrices are too expensive in memory and computation [ts=3819s]. The practical advice is that Adam or AdamW is a strong default, AdamW with warmup and cosine decay is common, SGD+Momentum can outperform Adam with more tuning, and small full-batch problems may justify second-order methods [ts=4006s]. The lecture closes by previewing neural networks: a 2-layer network uses two weight matrices with a nonlinearity such as ReLU between them, enabling transformations that can make non-linearly separable points separable by a final linear classifier [ts=4044s].

key_points.text: Learning rate is a hyperparameter for SGD, SGD+Momentum, RMSProp, Adam, and AdamW.
Too-high learning rates can explode or bounce around minima; too-low learning rates converge slowly.
Step decay reduces learning rate at fixed points, such as multiplying by 0.1 during ResNet training.
Cosine decay follows a half-cosine schedule from maximum learning rate toward zero.
Linear warmup ramps learning rate from zero to a target maximum before continuing with a scheduler.
Linear scaling rule: if batch size increases by N, increase learning rate by N.
Second-order optimization uses the Hessian to build a quadratic approximation but is impractical for very large deep networks.
A 2-layer neural network inserts a nonlinearity such as ReLU between two weight matrices.

kp_catalog.name: Adam and AdamW
kp_catalog.description: Adam combines momentum and RMSProp-style adaptivity; AdamW decouples weight decay from moment estimation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because adam and AdamW formalizes how adam combines momentum and RMSProp-style adaptivity; AdamW decouples weight decay from moment estimation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Learning-rate schedules, warmup, and batch-size scaling
kp_catalog.description: Changing learning rates during training using step, cosine, linear, inverse-square-root schedules, warmup, and linear scaling with batch size.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because learning-rate schedules, warmup, and batch-size scaling clarifies changing learning rates during training using step, cosine, linear, inverse-square-root schedules, warmup, and linear scaling with batch size, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Second-order optimization and Hessian limitations
kp_catalog.description: Using Hessians and quadratic approximations, and why these methods are usually impractical for large deep networks.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because second-order optimization and Hessian limitations extends the core thread to using Hessians and quadratic approximations, and why these methods are usually impractical for large deep networks without being required for the main pipeline.

kp_catalog.name: Neural-network nonlinearity preview
kp_catalog.description: A two-layer neural network adds a nonlinearity between weight matrices to transform data into a linearly separable representation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because neural-network nonlinearity preview formalizes how a two-layer neural network adds a nonlinearity between weight matrices to transform data into a linearly separable representation, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
